sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — SNES / Super Mario World
sources.notes: Primary community resource for movement constants and tricks. Documents speed values in sub-pixels/frame.

sources.name: SMW Central — RAM Map
sources.notes: Authoritative RAM address map from the SMW ROM hacking community. Jump table at ROM $00D2BD documented here.

sources.name: SMW Central — Quantifying Player Physics Thread
sources.notes: Community thread with empirically measured physics constants. Speed oscillation patterns documented.

sources.name: SMW Full Disassembly (Ersanio)
sources.notes: Full annotated 65816 disassembly of SMW ROM. Primary source for all ROM-level constants.

sources.name: Super Mario World Physics — Kenton Hamaluik
sources.notes: Real-unit physics analysis. Useful for cross-checking feel vs documented values.

notes: SMW uses 16 sub-pixels per pixel (4-bit fractional precision). Speed values are in sub-pixels/frame.
Tile size is 16x16 pixels (one 'block'). 16 blocks/pixel and 16 pixels/block form the SMW grid.
The jump speed table at ROM $00D2BD maps Mario's current X speed to a Y speed at jump initiation.
Speed values oscillate between frames due to the sub-pixel accumulation system — the exact value alternates each frame.
Positive Y = down (screen coordinates). Jump velocities are negative (upward).
NTSC SNES runs at 60.0988 fps, treated as 60fps in practice.
PAL SNES runs at 50fps. Physics constants ARE adjusted in the PAL version of SMW to compensate.

subpixel_system.description: SMW uses a 4-bit fractional position system. Speeds are in units of 1/16 pixel per frame. One sub-pixel = 0.0625 pixels.

display.notes: SNES outputs 256x224 visible pixels at standard settings. SMW tiles are 16x16 pixels.

walk.source: TASVideos SMW game resources — walking speed documented as oscillating between 35-37 sub-px/frame
walk.notes: Walk speed oscillates due to sub-pixel accumulation rounding. Average is approximately 36/16 = 2.25 px/frame. Walk state active when B is not held and P-meter below threshold.

run.notes: Run speed (B held but P-meter not full) has similar oscillation to walk. Run state allows P-meter to charge.

sprint.source: TASVideos: 'his speed varies between five different values (48, 47, 48, 47, 49)'
sprint.activation: B held with P-meter at max (value 112)
sprint.notes: Sprint (full P-meter) is the fastest ground state without Cape. Oscillates 47-49 sub-px/frame, avg ~48 = 3 px/frame.

p_meter.source: TASVideos: 'P-Meter Peak: 112 is the maximum value before sprinting begins'

takeoff_meter.purpose: Cape/Yoshi flight takeoff requires P-speed and this secondary meter
takeoff_meter.source: TASVideos: 'Takeoff Meter: Starts at 80 and decrements by 1 each frame'

flying_cape.source: TASVideos: 'Flying Speed: Top speed of 51, with oscillation of (51, 50, 49, 48, 47) every 5 frames'
flying_cape.notes: Cape flying max speed. Oscillation is 5-frame cycle. Slightly faster than sprint without cape.

swimming.source: TASVideos: swim oscillates 15-17 normally, 31-33 carrying item

jump._source: SMW ROM disassembly — jump table at ROM address $00D2BD
jump.jump_speed_calculation: Y speed at jump = table[floor(mario_x_speed / 8)]. Higher X speed = faster initial jump velocity.
notes: $B0 as unsigned byte = 176; as signed = -80. At 16 sub-pixels/pixel: 80/16 = 5.0 px/frame upward.
$B6 as unsigned = 182; as signed = -74. Spin jump: 74/16 = 4.625 px/frame upward.
Spin jump has lower initial velocity than normal jump — spin jumps are shorter but have a wider arc.
Jump speed INCREASES with X speed (running jumps go higher than standing jumps).
At max sprint speed the jump velocity is several sub-pixels higher than the $B0 base.
Holding A after jump initiation reduces gravity while ascending (variable-height jump).

corner_boost_y_speed_threshold.source: TASVideos: Y speed requirements for corner boost mechanics

notes: SMW gravity is variable — it depends on: whether A is held, jump type (normal vs spin), Mario's Y speed direction, and Mario's X speed at jump initiation.
Gravity while ascending and holding A is much lower than gravity while falling.
The game uses multiple gravity states: low-grav ascending (A held), normal ascending (A not held), falling.

holding_jump_ascending.notes: Approximate value — SMW low gravity while holding A is comparable to SMB1's holding-jump gravity.

releasing_jump_ascending.notes: Releasing A early produces faster upward deceleration, cutting jump height significantly.

falling.notes: Standard falling gravity. SMW has relatively floaty falling compared to later platformers.

notes: Yoshi's tongue extends 2 tiles (32px) forward.
Tongue must make contact with an enemy within the extension frames to capture it.
Yoshi can eat most enemies that fit in 1-2 tiles. Large enemies are spit out or not eatable.
Yoshi stomp on enemy: Yoshi jumps off with normal jump velocity.
Cape spin: 8-frame window at activation. Used for attacking enemies.
Cape glide: holding A while descending dramatically reduces fall rate.
Cape fly: requires P-meter full AND holding A on the way up from a running jump.
Cape-running speed at max (51 sub-px/frame) is slightly faster than foot-sprint (49 max).

small_mario.notes: Small Mario is 1 tile tall (16px), slightly narrower than a tile wide.

hitboxes.notes: SMW hitboxes are slightly narrower than sprites — there is 4px of visual 'forgiveness' on each side.

notes: One SMW block = 16x16 pixels.
Standard level height is typically 27 blocks (432px) including sky and underground sections.
Horizontal scrolling levels can extend hundreds of blocks.
Vertical levels use upward scrolling.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: SMW's speed oscillation is a real behavior from sub-pixel accumulation — the renderer will see the character moving at non-integer speeds. Implement sub-pixel accumulation correctly.
The P-meter system is the core flow mechanic: building speed for a sustained run before a big jump or takeoff. Critical to preserve when building a SMW-inspired platformer.
Jump height variability (based on X speed at jump time) is a key skill expression mechanism. Standing jumps vs running jumps have meaningfully different arcs.
Spin jumps have lower max height but wider safe-fall zone — they're the cautious player's tool.
The Cape is the most complex subsystem — gliding, spinning, flying all have separate physics. Start with walk/run/jump before adding Cape.
